amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: Chromatin silencing in S. cerevisiae represses transcription at telomeres, HM loci (silent mating type), and rDNA through formation of heterochromatin-like structures. Sir proteins (Sir2 NAD+-dependent histone deacetylase, Sir3, Sir4) assemble at silencers, spread along chromatin by binding hypoacetylated histones, and recruit additional Sir proteins creating silent domains. Rap1 protein nucleates silencing. The process requires multiple AND gates: all Sir proteins must be present, NAD+ available for Sir2 activity, and proper chromatin context.

name: Chromatin Silencing at Telomeres and HM Loci
notes: Chromatin silencing shows very high AND gates (10) for Sir complex assembly and spreading. Sir2 is NAD+-dependent deacetylase linking metabolism to epigenetics.
scientificAccuracy: Based on decades of research on yeast silencing. All Sir proteins and mechanisms validated.

sources.title: Sir2 is a NAD-dependent protein deacetylase.
sources.authors: Imai, S.; Armstrong, C. M.; Kaeberlein, M.; Guarente, L.

sources.title: A chromatin-silencing pathway mediating transcriptional repression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
sources.authors: Rusche, L. N.; Kirchmaier, A. L.; Rine, J.

sources.title: The establishment, inheritance, and function of silent chromatin domains.
sources.authors: Rusche, L. N.; Kirchmaier, A. L.; Rine, J.
